name: Clearwater River
short_description: BLM public land along the scenic Clearwater River offering agate and jasper collecting on gentle slopes. Free rockhounding with a 25 lb daily limit.
description:
The Clearwater River area in central Idaho provides excellent rockhounding opportunities on public BLM land at an elevation of 1,285 feet. This region sits within the Idaho Batholith geological province, where ancient granitic intrusions and surrounding metamorphic rocks have weathered to expose quality agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. The gentle slope terrain makes this site accessible for rockhounders of varying experience levels, though you will need sturdy footwear for rocky creek beds and scattered outcrops.

The Clearwater River drainage has been a productive area for rockhounders for decades, with specimens found both in the active riverbed and in adjacent gravels and weathered slopes. Spring snowmelt can temporarily increase water flow, so late spring through fall offers the most stable collecting conditions. The area supports light vegetation typical of mid-elevation Idaho forests, providing some shade during warmer months.

Because this is BLM land, there are no fees or permits required for recreational collecting. You are limited to 25 pounds per day per person for personal use only. The gentle slope and proximity to the river make this an ideal location for families and beginners learning to identify quality specimens in their natural context. Experienced rockhounders often focus on the older terraces and weathered outcrops slightly upslope from the active channel.
history: The Clearwater River valley lies within the Idaho Batholith, a massive granitic intrusion that formed during the Cretaceous Period when subduction zone magmatism created the core of central Idaho. As these granitic and metamorphic rocks weathered over millions of years, silica-rich fluids precipitated agates, jaspers, and chalcedony in fractures and vugs, with specimens subsequently concentrated in river gravels by Holocene erosion.
tips: Search the active riverbed and recent gravel deposits along the Clearwater River where lighter-colored agate and jasper specimens are often easy to spot against darker river cobbles. Focus on areas where the river has cut into older terraces and cut banks, which frequently expose weathered specimens. Bring a small hand lens and classifier to examine specimens for the translucent banding characteristic of quality agate - look for pieces showing color variation from white to red to brown. A 5-gallon bucket and small chisel are useful for working loose specimens from decomposing host rock along the banks.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1,285 feet with gentle slopes leading to the Clearwater River. Rocky terrain with scattered large boulders and cobbles typical of river country. Walking distance from parking to best collecting areas is typically under half a mile. Footing is uneven but manageable; wear sturdy boots with good ankle support. Seasonal variation in water level means some areas may be inaccessible during high spring flow.
best_season: May - October. Late spring through early fall offers stable water levels and comfortable temperatures. Avoid April when snowmelt can swell the river, and November through March when high water and cold make access difficult.
written_directions: From Stites, take Idaho State Highway 13 south toward Kooskia for approximately 8 miles. Look for informal pullouts and access points along the river corridor where the road approaches the Clearwater River. The BLM land is directly accessible from several roadside locations; park safely off the highway and walk down to the riverbed and adjacent slopes. Exact pullout locations vary seasonally based on water levels and road maintenance.
road_conditions: Idaho State Highway 13 is paved. Informal pullouts are unpaved dirt or gravel, well-maintained in summer but may have washboard texture. Standard passenger vehicles suitable.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along Highway 13 near the river. Limited dedicated parking; pull safely off roadway. Capacity varies by location, typically 2-4 vehicles per pullout.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or mechanical equipment. Respect private property boundaries and posted closures.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per person
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or power tools
No collection on private property adjacent to BLM land
Leave area clean; pack out all trash
nearest_services: Stites, 8 miles north, has basic services. Kooskia, 15 miles south, offers more services including gas, food, and lodging.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy access for most hikers. Uneven rocky terrain with stream crossings possible. No wheelchair access to prime collecting areas. Sturdy footwear required due to loose rocks and cobbles. Some slopes are steep in places; overall terrain is manageable for reasonably fit adults and children 6 and older.

faq.question: What gems can you find at Clearwater River near Stites, Idaho?
faq.answer: Clearwater River is known for producing quality agate, jasper, chalcedony, and quartz specimens. The agates often display attractive banding in white, red, and brown colors. Specimens range from small pebbles suitable for polishing to larger pieces for cutting and display.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Clearwater River BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on this BLM land. It is free to collect, with only a 25 lb daily limit per person for personal use. Commercial collection is not allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound the Clearwater River in Idaho?
faq.answer: May through October is ideal, with late spring and early fall providing stable water levels and comfortable temperatures. Avoid April when snowmelt swells the river, and November through March when high water and cold weather make access difficult and dangerous.

faq.question: Is Clearwater River a good rockhounding spot for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Clearwater River is excellent for beginners. The gentle slopes and easy access from roadside pullouts require no special equipment or permits. Specimens are relatively easy to spot in river gravels, making it ideal for learning to identify agate and jasper in their natural setting.

faq.question: What tools do you need to collect agate and jasper at Clearwater River?
faq.answer: A hand lens to examine specimens for quality, a small chisel for working loose rocks, a 5-gallon bucket, and a classifier are useful. Sturdy boots and gloves protect against sharp rocks. Most specimens can be collected by hand from loose gravel and river deposits.

faq.question: Can you take your dog rockhounding at Clearwater River?
faq.answer: Yes, dogs are welcome on this BLM public land. Keep your dog under control near the river, especially during high water when currents can be dangerous. The rocky terrain is suitable for dogs with sturdy paws and good footing.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding at Clearwater River near Stites. Find agate, jasper, and quartz. No permit needed. 25 lb daily limit. Easy access.
